cards.text: The process of checking whether user input conforms to what the program is expecting is called {{c1::input validation}}.
A program that handles error cases well is said to be {{c1::robust}}.

cards.concept: The three basic ways to do input validation
cards.explanation:
There are three approaches to validate input:
  • Inline (as user types): Prevent invalid input character-by-character (not supported by std::cin)
  • Post-entry (after user types): Let user enter string, validate it, then convert to final format
  • Let std::cin extract and handle errors: Easiest method - let operator>> try to extract, then handle failures
The third method is most common and easiest to implement.
examples: // Inline: GUI validates each keystroke (std::cin doesn't support)

cards.concept: The 4 types of invalid text input errors
cards.explanation:
Input errors can be categorized into four types:
  • Meaningless input: Extraction succeeds but input doesn't make sense (e.g., 'k' for a math operator)
  • Extraneous input: Extraction succeeds but user enters extra characters (e.g., '*q hello' for operator)
  • Extraction fails: Can't extract to variable type (e.g., 'q' into numeric input)
  • Numeric overflow: Extraction succeeds but value overflows the type's range

cards.text: To ignore all characters up to and including the next newline, call {{c1::std::cin.ignore(std::numeric_limits<std::streamsize>::max(), '\n')}}.

cards.question: What does this helper function do?
cards.answer: Clears all characters from std::cin's buffer up to and including the next newline character.
cards.explanation: This function wraps the long std::cin.ignore() call into a convenient helper that removes extraneous input from the buffer.

cards.text: When std::cin enters failure mode, {{c1::std::cin.clear()}} must be called to put it back in normal operation mode, then {{c2::ignoreLine()}} to remove the bad input.

cards.front: How do you detect if a prior std::cin extraction failed?
cards.back: Check if (!std::cin) or if (std::cin.fail()). Both test if the previous extraction was unsuccessful.

cards.front: What happens if you call std::cin.ignore() while std::cin is in failure mode?
cards.back: It will silently fail and do nothing. You must call std::cin.clear() first to put std::cin back in normal operation mode.

cards.question: What pattern does this function implement?
cards.answer: Input validation loop: keeps asking until valid input is entered.
cards.explanation: This function loops until the user enters valid input. If extraction fails, it clears the error state, removes bad input, and tries again. If successful, it removes any extraneous input and returns the value.

cards.text: In C++, EOF is an {{c1::error state}}, not a character. When std::cin encounters EOF and the input stream may be {{c2::disconnected}}, you should typically {{c3::std::exit(0)}} to terminate the program.

cards.front: How do you detect if the user entered an EOF character?
cards.back: Check if (std::cin.eof()) returns true after a failed extraction.

cards.question: What does this helper function do?
cards.answer: Returns true if there is unextracted input (other than newline) remaining in std::cin's buffer.
cards.explanation: This checks if we've extracted all input from the current line. If not at EOF and the next character isn't newline, there's extraneous input.

cards.front: What happens when you extract a value that's too large for a numeric type (e.g., 40000 into std::int16_t)?
cards.back: std::cin goes into failure mode and assigns the closest in-range value to the variable (e.g., 32767 for std::int16_t). This should be handled the same as other extraction failures.
